title: How the trigger of being ignored differs from being yelled at
author: Kalesh
excerpt:

Two different kinds of pain 

 I used to think all triggers were the same. A loud voice, a slammed door, a sudden shift in someone's face. I'd feel that f...
htmlContent:
Two different kinds of pain

I used to think all triggers were the same. A loud voice, a slammed door, a sudden shift in someone's face. I'd feel that familiar spike in my chest, the heat rising, the urge to either fight or disappear. But over time I started noticing something. Being yelled at and being ignored felt completely different in my body. Not just a little different. , neurologically different. Like two separate languages of threat that my nervous system had learned to speak fluently.

Here's the thing. Stephen Porges, the researcher who developed Polyvagal Theory, would tell you that both experiences activate your nervous system's defense responses. But they activate different branches of it. Being yelled at often triggers the sympathetic nervous system. The fight or flight response. Your heart rate jumps, your muscles tense, your breath gets shallow. You feel the energy building, ready to either push back or run. It's a hot activation. You can feel it burning through you.

Being ignored, though. That's different. That's cold. That activates what Porges calls the dorsal vagal response. The freeze response. The collapse response. Your body says, "There's no point in fighting or fleeing. They don't see me. I don't exist." And your system shuts down. Your voice gets stuck in your throat. Your thoughts go foggy. You feel heavy, numb, like you're disappearing into the floor.

I've had clients tell me they'd rather be screamed at than ignored. I understand that completely. At least when someone yells, you know they see you. You're registering on their radar. Being ignored is a different kind of wound. It's the wound of invisibility. The message your nervous system receives is not "I'm in danger" but "I don't matter." And that cuts deeper for many of us, especially those of us with childhood emotional neglect.

The body remembers what the mind tries to forget

Bessel van der Kolk, who wrote "The Body Keeps the Score," spent decades showing us that trauma lives in the body. Not just in our memories or our thoughts. In our tissues, our nervous system, our physical responses to the world. And the way your body remembers being yelled at is different from how it remembers being ignored.

When you were yelled at as a child, your body learned to brace. To tense up. To prepare for impact. Maybe the yelling was followed by physical punishment. Maybe it was just the sheer force of the sound that felt like an assault. Either way, your nervous system encoded that experience as a threat that requires mobilization. Your body learned to get ready to defend itself.

A book that many trauma survivors find genuinely helpful is The Body Keeps the Score (paid link). Van der Kolk walks through exactly how different types of trauma imprint on the body. Reading it helped me understand why my shoulders would shoot up to my ears every time someone raised their voice. Why my jaw would clench. Why my hands would ball into fists without me even noticing. That was my body's memory of being yelled at, playing out in real time.

But being ignored leaves a different kind of scar. When you were ignored as a child, your body learned to disappear. To go quiet. To stop asking for what you needed because no one was listening anyway. That's not a fight response. That's a collapse. Your nervous system said, "If I make myself small enough, maybe I won't feel the pain of being unseen."

I see this in my own body when I'm triggered by being ignored. I don't get tense. I get still. My breathing slows. My gaze goes distant. I feel like I'm watching myself from far away. It's a dissociative response. And dissociation, as van der Kolk explains, is one of the most common and misunderstood responses to trauma. It's not weakness. It's a survival strategy your body developed to endure something unbearable.

The nervous system doesn't care about logic

Here's where it gets tricky. Your nervous system doesn't distinguish between a genuine threat and a perceived one. It doesn't care that your partner is just tired and not actually abandoning you. It doesn't care that your boss is distracted and not intentionally ignoring you. The moment it detects a pattern that matches an old wound, it activates the same response it did when you were five years old.

So when someone raises their voice, even slightly, your body might respond as if you're about to be attacked. Even if that person has never hurt you. Even if you logically know they're just excited or passionate. Your nervous system doesn't wait for logic. It reacts first, asks questions later.

And when someone goes quiet, or looks away, or doesn't respond to your text, your body might respond as if you've ceased to exist. Even if that person is just busy or introverted or processing their own stuff. The trigger of being ignored bypasses your rational mind entirely and goes straight to that old wound of invisibility.

One resource that often resonates deeply with C-PTSD survivors is Complex PTSD: From Surviving to Thriving (paid link). Pete Walker, the author, breaks down the different types of trauma responses and how they show up in relationships. He talks about the freeze response in a way that finally made sense to me. It's not just about being still. It's about the deep, bone-level belief that you have no agency. That your voice doesn't matter. That you can't change your circumstances.

Think about it. When you're triggered by being yelled at, you might feel angry. You might want to yell back or run away. You still feel like a person who can act. But when you're triggered by being ignored, you might feel nothing. Emptiness. You might not even realize you're triggered until hours later when the shame creeps in. That's the freeze response doing its work. Keeping you small. Keeping you quiet. Keeping you safe from the pain of being seen and rejected.

Healing looks different for each trigger

This is where the wellness industry often gets it wrong. They treat all triggers the same. Take a deep breath. Use a grounding technique. Repeat an affirmation. But that approach misses the nuance. What works for a hot trigger like being yelled at might not work for a cold trigger like being ignored.

When I'm triggered by yelling, I need to move. I need to shake off that energy. I need to let my body know that it's safe to release the tension. I might go for a walk, do some jumping jacks, or just stand up and stretch. The goal is to complete the fight or flight cycle that got interrupted. To let my body know that the threat has passed and I can come back to regulation.

A breathing tool that helps slow the exhale for nervous system regulation is Breathing Exercise Tool (paid link). It guides you into a longer exhale, which activates the vagus nerve and signals safety to the nervous system. I use it when I'm in that hot, activated state. It helps me come back down without forcing myself to calm down before my body is ready.

But when I'm triggered by being ignored, movement alone doesn't help. I need connection. I need to feel seen. I need to remind my nervous system that I exist and that I matter. That might mean calling a friend. Writing in a journal. Looking at myself in the mirror and saying my name out loud. It might mean doing something that creates a sensory experience of my own presence. Taking a warm shower. Putting my hand on my heart. Feeling my feet on the floor.

For understanding emotional neglect specifically, a helpful resource is Running on Empty (paid link). Jonice Webb writes about how childhood emotional neglect creates this deep wound of invisibility. Reading it helped me understand why being ignored feels so catastrophic to my system. It's not just about the present moment. It's about every moment in my childhood when I needed someone to see me and no one did.

Wild, right? The same nervous system that protects you from physical danger is also trying to protect you from the pain of being unseen. And it's doing its best with the tools it has. But those tools were developed in a different time, for a different situation. And now they're showing up in your adult relationships, causing reactions that feel disproportionate to what's actually happening.

You can learn to respond instead of react

I'm not going to tell you that you'll never be triggered again. That's not how this works. Triggers are part of being human, especially if you have a history of trauma. But you can learn to recognize the difference between being triggered by yelling and being triggered by being ignored. And that recognition changes everything.

When you know that being yelled at activates your sympathetic nervous system, you can choose to move your body. To shake, to walk, to release that energy. You can tell yourself, "This is a hot trigger. I need to discharge before I can think clearly."

When you know that being ignored activates your dorsal vagal response, you can choose to reconnect. To reach out, to ground yourself, to remind your nervous system that you exist. You can tell yourself, "This is a cold trigger. I need to feel my presence before I can feel safe."

Sit with that. Notice what just happened in your body as you read those words. Did you feel a shift? A recognition? A sense of relief at having language for something you've felt your whole life?

That's the beginning of healing. Not the end. Just the beginning. Because once you understand how your nervous system works, you can stop fighting it. You can stop judging yourself for having these reactions. You can start working with your body instead of against it.

And that's the real work. Not eliminating triggers. But learning to meet them with compassion, curiosity, and the right tools. Your nervous system isn't broken. It's doing exactly what it was designed to do. It's protecting you from pain it remembers too well. And with time, patience, and the right support, it can learn new ways to respond. Ways that don't require you to disappear or to brace for impact. Ways that let you stay present, stay connected, and stay whole.

Healing Journey Resources

  • The Body Keeps the Score (paid link) - Bessel van der Kolk's essential work on how trauma lives in the body
  • Complex PTSD: From Surviving to Thriving (paid link) - Pete Walker's guide to understanding trauma responses
  • Breathing Exercise Tool (paid link) - A device to help regulate the nervous system through extended exhale breathing
  • Running on Empty (paid link) - Jonice Webb's book on childhood emotional neglect and its lasting effects
